back: A schedule where, for every pair of transactions Ti and Tj such that Tj reads a value written by Ti, the commit operation of Ti appears before the commit operation of Tj. This ensures no committed transaction reads data from an uncommitted transaction that later aborts.

front: What is a cascading rollback (or cascading abort)?
back: A situation where the abort (rollback) of a single transaction forces the abort of other transactions that have read data written by the aborted transaction. This phenomenon is undesirable as it leads to wasted work.

front: What is a cascadeless schedule (or avoid cascading aborts schedule)?
back: A schedule where transactions only read data written by transactions that have already been committed. This property prevents cascading rollbacks, as no transaction ever reads uncommitted data.

A schedule where transactions neither read nor write data items that have been written by an uncommitted transaction. This is a stronger condition than cascadeless (which only restricts reads) and simplifies recovery by allowing the recovery manager to undo transactions using only the before-images of data items.

front: How do the strictness properties relate to each other?
back: All strict schedules are cascadeless. All cascadeless schedules are recoverable. Therefore: Strict => Cascadeless => Recoverable. A schedule can be recoverable but not cascadeless, or cascadeless but not strict.
